data.title: From Mining To Megawatts -- Core Scientific's High-Density Strategy Unfolds
data.description:
In our previous report we argued the investment case of Core Scientific. We highlighted how CORZ transformed itself from a struggling bitcoin miner to a key AI -Infrastructure with the help of the contracts and capex commitments that they secured form CoreWeave. We also highlighted why this investment opportunity existed, as two major events disrupted the stock price with one disrupting the whole AI sector and the other one impacting only Core Scientific. First news was the DeepSeek announcement - a new AI framework claiming significantly lower development costs and computation needs. This was refuted by the industry experts. The demand for AI computational power was further reaffirmed when CoreWeave announced a new contract with OpenAI. The second news of Microsoft pulling back on some commitments with CoreWeave also turned out to be false. After a stellar CoreWeave debut on the markets and no news of Microsoft contract cancellations it is clear that the contracts of CORZ with CoreWeave are secure. This was further reaffirmed in this earnings when CORZ confirmed that its contracts with CoreWeave are “Take-or-pay” and there is no ability to unilaterally terminate the contracts. This will be discussed in more detail as we go through the earnings.

Core Scientific’s Q1 2025 earnings reflected a strategic pivot from digital asset mining to becoming a dominant player in AI driven, high density colocation infrastructure. The quarter underscored rapid operational progress, strong strategic poisoning, and expanding enterprise demand amid a foundational partnership with CoreWeave.

CEO Adam Sullivan emphasized that Corz is at the forefront of one of the most significant shifts in digital infrastructure specifically designed for high performance computing, particularly GPU based workloads essential for AI and machine learnings. Corz is not merely participating in but leading the transformation. The company is currently the only pure play public infrastructure provider solely focused on high density colocation which gives it a unique competitive advantage.

CEO Sullivan also note that AI infrastructure demand remains robust, countering media narratives suggesting a softening. Traditional datacentres are struggling to keep up with the density requirements that AI workload demands. Core Scientific is capitalizing on that supply demand mismatch which gives them a real opportunity to lead and to carve out a meaningful share in the market that’s just getting started.

CoreWeave Partnership as a strategic growth engine

At the centre of core scientific growth strategy is its contracts with CoreWeave. Described as one of the largest GPU infrastructure agreements in the market, After a financial times article which claimed strained relationship between Microsoft and CoreWeave there was a huge debate about the nature of the contracts that Core Scientific has with CoreWeave. This quarter it has been clarified that these are take or pay contracts. This take or pay fixed price deal secures long term recurring revenue while keeping Core Scientific’s capital outlay minimal. CoreWeave is funding nearly all CapEx allowing Core Scientific to scale without overleveraging their balance sheet. The clarification around the CoreWeave contracts has eliminated the risk of contract cancellations if the computational demand is low.

The Denton, Texas facility emerged as the crown jewel of this partnership. Once completed, Denton will potentially house one of the largest GPU clusters in North America. The facility is already operational with 8MW delivered in MAY and additional 40MW expected in by Quarter end. CEO Adam Sullivan framed the speed of execution-achieving this within roughly four months of breaking ground-as a key differentiator.

Notably, OpenAI has been reported to be working with CoreWeave at Denton, underscoring the strategic importance and credibility of the site.

Strategic Goals

The company has announced three strategic priorities that are going to act as very positive catalysts as these are achieved.

Diversification of Customer Base

A core pillar of Core Scientific’s forward-looking strategy has been defined as reducing dependency on CoreWeave. Currently, CoreWeave represents a substantial portion of billable capacity, but the company aims to bring this below 50% by 2028. To that end, Core Scientific is actively expanding its customer pipeline.

While no new customer has been signed yet, the pipeline includes hyperscalers and significantly, a new front: large enterprises exploring AI infrastructure for the first time. Sullivan noted that enterprises clients are advancing more quickly through the decision-making cycle then hyper scalers and could become anchor tenants for future site developments. These customers are considering deployments or 50-100 MW, with a potential to grow beyond that, rivalling CoreWeave in scale over time. CoreWeave’s presence is a big credibility booster for these deals. Successful execution in Denton and other sites is expected to build trust and drive confidence across the market.

Executing on the CoreWeave contracts

Matt Brown, COO provided detailed updates on the construction progress. The company is simultaneously developing 570 MW across dour sties: Denton, Dalton, Marble, and Muskogee. These projects represent a combined infrastructure investment exceeding %5 billion.

At Denton, just to get an idea of the construction scale, over 600 contractors have logged 187,000-man hours poured 2800+ cubic yards of concrete, and installed over 30 miles of conduit. The company has already secured all equipment necessary to meet its 2025 delivery goals and is insulated from global tariff changes due to early procurement.

Looking ahead, Core scientific expects deliver 250 MW to CoreWeave by the end of 2025 and 590 MW by early 2027.

Expanding Data Centre Capacity

The company is targeting 300 MW of organic expansion across existing sites by 2027, supported by strong relationships with local utilities. An additional 400 is expected from new sites under development over the next three years.

Core Scientific is also exploring strategic M&A opportunities to extend its leadership in high density colocation. While most targets are expected to align with the company’s GPY focused assets o diversify workloads. The aim is to create a balanced portfolio while preserving efficiency and long-term value creation.

Notably, the Grand Forks site in North Dakota is being prepared for high density use, The company recently acquired 70 adjacent acres, with interest already emerging from hyperscalers, content providers, and enterprise clients.

The New CFO Jim Nygaard has deep experience in M&A, and capital markets. His addition is a very positive step in achieving the strategic expansion of data centre capacities through M&A’s.

Financial Strategy, Performance, & Tariffs

New CFO Jim Nygaard, replacing Denise Sterling, used the call to share his perspective on capital discipline. CoreWeave’s capex contributions have allowed core scientific to maintain a light balance sheet. As of Q1 the company held 780 million in liquidity including bitcoin. The long-term target for net debt to adjusted EBITDA is 4x consistent with peers.

Capital Deployment will be focused on:

  • Expanding capacity at existing sties,
  • Developing new high-density campuses, and
  • Targeted M&A aligned with strategic goals

Revenue for Q1 2025 was $79.5 million, a 16% sequential decline due to site conversions from bitcoin mining to colocation. Adjusted EBITDA came in at -$6.1m. The company mined 719 bitcoins in Q1 versus 974 in Q4 2024. The company also confirmed it ill stop reporting monthly bitcoin production, reflecting its shift away from mining. The focus now is on reporting construction progress tied to colocation projects.

The company also addressed the potential impacts of tariffs on data centre equipment. Management expects a 5% to 10% increase in CapEx for new builds, but believes the lease rates will rise proportionally, preserving margins. Core Scientifics early procurement strategy has largely insulated its 2025 plans from these impacts.

Interesting the company sees tariffs as a competitive advantage, likely to raise barriers for late entrant and further solidify its leadership in the high-density segment.

Conclusion

Core Scientific’s Q1 2025 results demonstrated a company in the midst of a bold and effective transformation. With the decline of its legacy Bitcoin mining business, CORZ is successfully repositioning itself as a high-impact infrastructure provider for the AI era. The company has not only identified a massive market opportunity but has taken decisive steps to lead it secured through strategic partnerships, accelerated project execution, and disciplined capital management.

The CoreWeave relationship remains the cornerstone of this transformation, and this quarter has put to rest the key investor concerns regarding contract durability and financial exposure. The take-or-pay structure, coupled with minimal capital outlay by Core Scientific, means the company can scale rapidly with limited balance sheet risk. Execution at the Denton facility further supports CORZ’s claim of being a first mover with operational capability in deploying large-scale, high-density GPU cluster.

Equally important is the company’s forward-looking strategy to diversify its customer base. With large enterprises now emerging as serious buyers of AI infrastructure, Core Scientific is building a healthy pipeline that could rival the scale of its CoreWeave engagement. Success here would not only reduce revenue concentration risk but also further elevate CORZ’s position as a mission critical infrastructure provider.

Financially, while Q1 shows a revenue dip and negative EBITDA due to the mining to colocation transition, the company maintains strong liquidity and is deploying capital with precision. The proactive approach to mitigate tariffs impacts, paired with a clear M&A roadmap under new CFO Jim Nygaard, gives confidence in the company’s ability to sustain long term capital efficient growth.

In short, Core Scientific is no longer a turnaround story. It is now a growth platform with deep exposure to one of the most transformative trends in technology. For investors seeking scalable AI infrastructure exposure with asymmetric upside potential, CORZ remains a compelling and timely opportunity. The clarity provided this quarter on the durability of the CoreWeave contracts, coupled with company’s execution strength and accelerating enterprise demand, has only reinforced our investment thesis. Our valuation framework and long position in Core Scientific remain firmly intact.

data.title: Nvidia: China Is Not The AI Problem
data.description:
Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) - the dominant force in data center acceleration and AI infrastructure - delivered strong Q1 2026 earnings, beating Wall Street expectations on both revenue and earnings. Despite tightening U.S. export controls on AI chip sales to China, the company demonstrated operational resilience and continued to advance its focused and diversified AI growth strategy.

In a previous report, I highlighted the strategic role of semiconductors in global trade and in an April article, I flagged the escalating U.S.-China trade tensions as a risk to Nvidia’s outlook. Nevertheless, I have maintained my Buy rating on Nvidia stock based on the company’s leadership in the data center market. Since that update, Nvidia stock has surged 21.5% outperforming the S&P 500 (SP500). In this report, I analyze the Q1 2026 earnings and raise my price target to reflect Nvidia’s continued strength amid geopolitical and macroeconomic headwinds.

Nvidia Sales Momentum Continues Despite H20 China Write Off

Year-on-year, revenue grew 69% to $44.1 billion marking a 12% sequential increase to sales. Data center sales grew 73.3% year-on-year and 9.9% sequentially. This growth was realized despite new export controls on the H20 GPU, which is specially designed for the Chinese market. During the quarter this brought a $2.5 billion headwind to sales and since there is no demand for these chips elsewhere the company had to recognize a $4.5 billion charge resulting in a drag of roughly 10 points on the margins.

Gaming revenues grew 47.9% sequentially and 42.2% year-on-year to $3.76 billion driven by Blackwell adoption from gamers, creators and AI enthusiasts. Professional and visualization sales remained stable sequentially at $509 million but up 19.2% year-on-year driven by Ada RTX adoption. Automotive sales remained stable sequentially at $567 million but were up 72.3% year-on-year. Other sales declined 11.9% to $111 million but were up 42.3% year-on-year.

Adjusted gross margins were down sequentially and year-on-year, which is a function of Blackwell ramp up and excess inventory and purchase obligations on products other than the H20. The charge and lower H20 sales resulted in a 9% sequential drop in operating income to $23.3 billion but still up 29%.

Nvidia Provides Q2 2026 Earnings Outline

For Q2 2026, the company expects $45 billion in sales at the midpoint with a 2% variation in either direction. The mid-point of the range is below analyst estimates of $45.6 billion but we do note that Nvidia has a strong history on beating revenue expectations. The outlook includes a $8 billion drag from H20 revenues that will not occur. Margins are expected to be around 72% driven by improvement in Blackwell margins. The company is working towards achieving mid-70s margins.

So, the company expects sequential growth in revenues, but the impact of the export controls is significant. Absent of the controls for the H20, H20 Q1 sales would have been $7.1 billion and Q2 sales would have been $8 billion. The overarching issue is that the market in China is worth $50 billion, and CEO Jensen Huang provided the following comment on that during the Q2 2025 earnings call:

On export control: China is one of the world's largest AI markets and a springboard to global success. With half of the world's AI researchers based there, the platform that wins China is positioned to lead globally. Today, however, the $50 billion China market is effectively closed to U.S. industry. The H20 export ban ended our Hopper Data Center business in China. We cannot reduce Hopper further to comply. As a result, we are taking a multibillion-dollar write-off on inventory that cannot be sold or repurposed. We are exploring limited ways to compete, but Hopper is no longer an option.

Those comments show the size of the market, but also the fact that right now Nvidia has nothing that it can provide to the Chinese market. The Hopper generation chips cannot be adapted for China to comply with export controls. While Nvidia says the market is effectively closed now, it is considering launching a GPU based on the Blackwell architecture for China. Given the market size it is to be expected that Nvidia wants to be in that market even if it can’t provide its best chips. However, the risk is simply that Nvidia even if it does make a GPU specifically for China that it may not be competitive and there is always the risk that the U.S. tightens export controls even more which once again may trigger a write.

Nvidia Stock Remains A Buy Amid China Concerns

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions, and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

I would not call the export controls on China to be overblown. We clearly see the impact in Q1 and Q2, and with the knowledge that the Chines market is worth $50 billion there definitely is some reason to be concerned because the export controls do hurt the business.

We also see that back in estimates with EBITDA estimates having come down by 7% for FY26 and by 5% and 3% in the years after. The free cash flow impact is smaller at 2% this year and 1% next year. It may indicate that China was already expected to be a smaller portion of the EBITDA and FCF pie or that over time we have some partially offset by sales in other regions.

I previously saw fundamental support for a $223.93 price target and $156 when allowing the stock to trade in between the implied stock price targets for the industry and the company median. If we do that again using the most recent estimates, we get to a $143 price target, which I deem conservative given that Nvidia has posted strong earnings despite the pressure on sales. Applying only half of the upside to materialize relative to the company EV/EBITDA implied price target for FY26 we get to a $170.85 price target which is now my baseline price target.

Conclusion: Nvidia Performs Well Despite Export Pressure

The Q1 2026 earnings were strong, and I would say that in some ways China is not Nvidia’s problem. The U.S. is a bigger problem for Nvidia. Given the sudden implementation of the tighter controls, Nvidia had no time to get rid of its H20 inventory and I would say that makes the fact that the U.S. curbs chip sales to other countries for understandable reasons a bigger threat. The export control environment is dynamic, but it has not provided Nvidia with the possibility to limit its losses caused by the export controls. It does make it an example of how a U.S. company is getting hurt and perhaps even hurt more by U.S. controls than China.

Nevertheless, the company remains on the forefront of AI driven data center growth. What remains to be seen is whether the company is willing to develop a new product for China at the risk that a next round of export control tightening renders the inventory worthless.

data.title: SentinelOne: Market Conditions To Blame For Poor Q1 Results
data.description:
SentinelOne's (NYSE:S) share price plunged after it reported surprisingly weak growth in the first quarter and lowered full year guidance. This is particularly disappointing given that the CrowdStrike (CRWD) outage should have been an opportunity for SentinelOne. I believe the current situation is as much due to market weakness as problems with SentinelOne's business though.

I previously suggested that SentinelOne's future was dependent on its ability to scale its emerging products. The stock is down close to 30% since then, with adoption of the company's expansion products continuing to disappoint. While SentinelOne has a solid portfolio of solutions and is reasonably priced, it lacks scale and competition is an issue. It is also hard to be constructive about the prospects of the stock while growth continues to decelerate.

Market Conditions

SentinelOne has suggested that elevated macro volatility in April led to longer sales cycles, negatively impacting net new ARR. The company hasn’t seen projects cancelled though and its win rate remains strong. This likely surprised investors given cybersecurity spend was expected to remain resilient. For example, BTIG has stated that demand checks have generally been positive. Demand for CrowdStrike’s products is reportedly strong, while feedback on SentinelOne was slightly less positive, which was attributed to competitive concerns.

While there have been signs that the macro environment is stabilizing after a difficult couple of years, I am not sure that this will benefit most cybersecurity vendors. The shift to next-gen solutions is maturing, and competition is increasing as platforms race to introduce comprehensive solutions. As part of the platformization drive, some vendors have also been offering aggressive incentives. As a result, weaker growth probably should be expected for most vendors, if for no other reason than recent policy uncertainty causing organizations to delay decision making. SentinelOne is not alone in its struggles either. Okta (OKTA) reported disappointing results in Q1 and Palo Alto Networks (PANW) recently achieved its smallest revenue beat in 18 months. Fortinet (FTNT) also missed guidance for the first time in over 18 months.

SentinelOne Business Updates

SentinelOne's Singularity platform now encompasses:

  • AI and Automation
  • Endpoint
  • Cloud
  • Data
  • Identity
  • Exposure Management
  • Threat Services

Expansion outside of endpoint protection is becoming a larger part of SentinelOne's growth formula. The company has suggested that it is increasing its market share across cloud, data and endpoint security. Growth in emerging products has been disappointing relative to CrowdStrike though and has been a large part of the reason that SentinelOne's revenue growth has continued to decelerate. Approximately 40% of SentinelOne's enterprise customers have adopted more than 3 solution categories and roughly 20% have adopted more than 4. In comparison, over 60% of CrowdStrike's customers have adopted 5 or more modules. While solution categories and modules aren't directly comparable, CrowdStrike is clearly having much more success driving broad platform adoption.

SentinelOne's data solutions exceeded $100 million ARR in Q1 and non-endpoint solutions now represent around half of quarterly bookings. Data ARR appears to be growing at around a 100% growth rate. Customer interest in scalable, cloud-native SIEM solutions is reportedly increasing at the moment as they can lower costs and enable customers to address threats in real-time.

Purple AI bookings were up by a triple digit percentage YoY in the first quarter and the product had more than a 25% attach rate across subscriptions sold in the quarter. SentinelOne has suggested that when customers adopt its basic AI solutions it lifts average deal size by around 25%.

Cloud security is an area where SentinelOne hasn't had as much success. The company introduced its unified cloud security suite in Q1, which combines cloud workload protection, security posture management, cloud detection and response, data and identity security, and AI security posture management. This comes after SentinelOne's acquisition of PingSafe in 2024. Google’s acquisition of Wiz highlights how the market is continuing to consolidate, and there is a belief that this will pressure point-based solutions. While it is incorrect to characterize SentinelOne as a point solution, it lacks the scale of the other large cybersecurity players.

Much of SentinelOne's current focus is on combining AI and data to improve protection and increase automation. Purple AI Auto Triage was recently made generally available, a solution that helps to investigate and remediate threats. Singularity Hyperautomation was also made generally available. It helps to provide automated workflow capabilities and to execute novel detection rules autonomously. While SentinelOne believes that the AI and automation capabilities of its platform are an advantage, the company lacks scale relative to competitors, which is important in terms of access to data.

SentinelOne also believes that autonomous security solutions are important to its partners. The company launched PartnerOne in the first quarter, a program for MSSPs, incident responders, VARs, and technology partners. It offers a combination of tools, resources and incentives and SentinelOne hopes that the program will help it to reach more customers.

SentinelOne achieved FedRAMP High authorization for endpoint and AI SIEM last year and Purple AI, CNAPP and Hyperautomation this year. While the company's government pipeline remains promising, there is increased scrutiny at the moment. There has also been some uncertainty around SentinelOne's government business due to Christopher Krebs' position at the company. Krebs was the CISA chief before President Trump removed him from the position. Trump issued an executive order in April requiring relevant agencies to suspend security clearances for individuals and entities associated with Krebs, which included SentinelOne. Krebs subsequently resigned from SentinelOne but it is not clear whether there will be lingering ill will toward the company.

Financial Analysis

SentinelOne generated $229 million in revenue in the first quarter, an increase of 23% YoY, while ARR was up 24% to $948 million. Remaining performance obligations increased 33% to $1.2 billion, supported by customers committing to longer term agreements.

SentinelOne expects $242 million revenue in the second quarter, an increase of 22% YoY. The company has also suggested that net new ARR will be up over 30% sequentially based on the improvement in demand seen in May. This would mean that net new ARR would still be down YoY and ARR growth would decelerate to around 22%. For the full year, the company expects $996 million revenue, also up 22%.

SentinelOne's customer count is reportedly at an all-time high, although the company stopped giving this data over 12 months ago, which presumably indicates weakness. Most of SentinelOne's end customers are reached through partners, limiting the utility of this metric.

The number of customers with more than $100,000 ARR increased 22% YoY to 1,459 in the first quarter. Average deal size was up by a double digit percentage in the quarter. While this growth is solid, the sequential increase was poor. This is important given that around 50% of SentinelOne's business each quarter comes from net new customers. Strong customer additions in the 2 previous quarters may have been related to CrowdStrike outage.

SentinelOne's gross profit margin continues to edge higher, exceeding 75% in Q1. While gross margins may continue to increase, there is probably limited room for further upside at this point, meaning an improvement in the bottom line will need to be driven by operating leverage.

SentinelOne's operating margin was -36% in the first quarter. Margin improvements have stalled as SentinelOne has increased its growth investments. While investments in AI and automation are expected to drive operational efficiency, but it is hard to see this so far. The company's free cash flow margin was approximately 5% over the past 12 months.

The number of SentinelOne job openings has surged higher in recent months, which will pressure margins if growth continues to decelerate. It seems unlikely that the increase in job openings is due to an expectation of stronger growth, which raises the question of whether SentinelOne has been under hiring in order to improve its margins.

Conclusion

SentinelOne's soft first quarter result have returned the stock to recent lows. In some ways this is not surprising, given that the cybersecurity market is maturing and the fact that companies like Palo Alto Networks have been offering significant incentives in order to drive platformization.

I previously felt that SentinelOne's revenue growth rate was likely to stabilize in the high 20% range, in part due to CrowdStrike's outage creating an opportunity. The boost from this appears to have been limited though, and based on SentinelOne's commentary around emerging products, its endpoint growth is likely low.

Investors have soured on SentinelOne relative to peers over the past 12 months. While the company's valuation could prove reasonable long-term, SentinelOne must stabilize its revenue growth and begin driving margins higher again. This will likely happen at some point, but I think that there could be further downside before this occurs.

data.title: Stora Enso: Rebuild Is Real, But The Recovery Isn't
data.description:
Thesis

Stora Enso Oyj (OTCQX:SEOAY), a long-established Nordic business in renewable materials and packaging, is trying to reinvent itself. The company is selling off assets, reshuffling its org chart, and doubling down on higher-growth product lines. It’s also leaning harder into vertical integration and cost control as it faces rising input prices and changing global demand. My analysis looks at whether those moves are enough to overcome ongoing profitability problems and actually deliver value in a sector where margins are getting tighter by the day.

Turning Timber into Liquidity

Since issuing my "Hold" recommendation on Stora Enso Oyj last winter, a stance that’s been affirmed by the stock’s near-flat trajectory, the company has presented two major developments that signal a deliberate reshaping of its strategic and financial foundations. Let’s start with the forestland transaction. This month, Stora Enso sold off 175,000 hectares of Swedish forestland, about 12.4% of its total Swedish forest portfolio, for €900 million, a price roughly aligned with book value. While that may appear at first blush to be a straightforward asset sale, the architecture of the deal reveals a subtler playbook. Stora Enso retains a 15% minority stake in the newly created forest entity. The controlling interest was handed to Soya Group and a MEAG-led consortium (Munich Re’s asset management arm). This is not an exit; it’s a partial externalization of a capital-intensive asset base, structured so as to maintain operational leverage. A 15-year renewable wood supply agreement, paired with a forest management service contract, ensures that Stora Enso keeps its hands on the steering wheel, even as it lets someone else push the financial cart.

Operationally, the divestiture will shave €25 million from annual EBITDA, €15 million of which is actual cash flow, but the tradeoff is liquidity. Specifically, the deal slashes net debt by €790 million, a critical maneuver considering that capital expenditure in Q1 2025 alone reached €240 million. Much of that Q1 spend was allocated to the tail end of the Oulu packaging board project, a €1 billion mega-investment that is only just beginning to produce.

Timing matters, especially when you’re sitting on a pile of forestland and trying to turn trees into cash. Monetizing those assets is about buying the ability to fund your own capex without groveling for outside capital. In this arena, turning slow-growth timber into fast-moving fiberboard margins is the whole point.

Which brings us to Oulu.

The new packaging board line, online since March 2025, is a 750,000-ton-per-year machine built for folding box board (FBB) and coated unbleached kraft (CUK), the kind of products regulators and green-tinged consumers now prefer over plastic. Think frozen food, dry goods, multipacks, the packaging backbone of your grocery aisle. At full speed, the site is expected to rake in €800 million a year by 2027. However, in the short term, management says they’ll break even on EBITDA by year-end, which means until then, the thing bleeds more than it boosts.

Still, the needle might be moving. Q1 2025 brought in €2.4 billion in net sales, up 9% year-over-year. Adjusted EBIT climbed to €175 million, an 18% improvement, and margins nudged up to 7.4%. It was the first time since Q3 2022 that every business unit posted a positive adjusted EBIT. For once, it looks like Oulu is starting to deliver, and volume plus pricing are finally doing what they’re supposed to do: make money.

Down the chain, Stora Enso’s been busy trying to lock down Oulu’s raw material diet. The site’s going to chew through 3.5 million cubic meters of wood a year, most of it from Finnish suppliers. To keep that pipeline steady, and cheap, they picked up Junnikkala sawmills, a move aimed squarely at securing low-cost wood chips and pulpwood. The rest comes from the Enocell and Veracel mills, tightening the loop into a neat regional circuit. It’s vertical integration with a blunt-force shield purpose against the kind of input price chaos that’s been hammering manufacturers for the last three years.

Margins Over Management

Stora Enso's organizational chart just went through the shredder too. The old five-division setup is gone, swapped out in early 2025 for a flatter structure: seven areas, 21 autonomous units, each one on the hook for its own profit and loss. One layer of management is axed. The idea is to pull leadership, especially the CEO, closer to the ground, where the customers and real problems live. Pay's been rejigged too. Sales and ops people now eat what they kill, so to speak, with bonuses tied to EBIT and gross margins.

Financially, the company’s housecleaning is producing visible results. The net debt-to-adjusted EBITDA ratio has declined to 3.2x, down from 4.0x a year earlier and the average interest rate on outstanding debt fell to 3.7% from 4.2% in Q1 2024, offering modest relief in a still-costly credit environment. Operating working capital was reduced by 3 percentage points, down to 7% of sales, reflecting tighter capital discipline. A €29 million foreign exchange tailwind also padded Q1 EBIT, though the sustainability of this FX boost is, of course, uncertain.

Even the forestland that Stora Enso has retained is proving a more valuable asset than before. As of Q1, the fair value of these holdings stood at €9.3 billion, translating to approximately €11.7 per share. This valuation supports the logic of selective monetization: by selling a portion while retaining core exposure, Stora Enso captures liquidity without surrendering optionality.

Externally, the company finds itself somewhat insulated from macro tremors like U.S. trade tariffs. In 2024, American sales comprised less than 3% of revenue, effectively neutralizing direct exposure to that particular geopolitical hazard. More interesting, though, is how Stora Enso interprets disruption as "opportunity." With U.S.-based exporters potentially sidelined, Stora Enso sees room to capture market share in key grades: 800,000 tonnes of cartonboard, 3.4 million tonnes of kraftliner, and 3.8 million tonnes of fluff pulp. In all three categories, the company is either the largest or among the top European producers. In other words, it is poised to plug global supply gaps left open by dislocated trade flows.

Big Projections, Weak Core

SEOAY's anchored by a forward price-to-earnings ratio of just 11.72, significantly compressed from a trailing P/E of 20.73 in 2024. On its face, this multiple compression implies a rebound in profitability is either underway or imminent, bolstered by consensus forecasts that call for 76.9% earnings per share growth heading into December 2025.

Layered atop that is a projected long-term EPS growth compound annual growth rate of 61.29% over the next 3–5 years, more than quadrupling the median for the materials sector. On these grounds, Stora Enso might appear dramatically undervalued. However, the glaring mismatch between this projected future and the company’s historical earnings profile reveals a tension that’s central to understanding the risk embedded in the stock.

Over the past five and ten years, Stora Enso has posted negative CAGRs for both EBITDA and operating income, down -7.71% and -12.5%, respectively. Even top-line revenue growth has been in decline over extended periods. This suggest deep-seated structural underperformance. Against this backdrop, forecasting rapid acceleration in earnings begins to feel more speculative than grounded.

This speculative quality is further underscored when you look at cash flow. On a headline level, cash from operations over the trailing twelve months stands at a healthy $970 million, which outpaces sector norms. But this number belies internal deterioration: it’s a step down from the company’s own five-year averages.

Even more troubling is the free cash flow margin, which has sunk into negative territory at -2.21%. What this implies is that the firm is not generating sufficient excess cash after capital expenditures, key metric for gauging a company’s ability to self-fund investments, return capital to shareholders, or reduce leverage. My take is that instead of becoming more self-sustaining, Stora Enso is dipping further into its capital reserves.

Spending Into the Red

These internal weaknesses are laid bare in its most recent earnings results. In Q1 2025, Stora Enso reported €192 million in operating cash flow, but a €100 million increase in working capital, largely tied to seasonal inventory build-up and the ramp-up of the Oulu mill’s new consumer packaging board line, offset much of that. Capital expenditure for the quarter was a substantial €240 million, leaving post-investment cash flow at a negative €47 million. The company indicated that CapEx may taper in subsequent quarters, but for now, it is a significant drain.

That Oulu ramp-up itself poses a direct threat to near-term earnings. Management disclosed that the ramp-up would impose a €100 million EBIT drag for full-year 2025, with €8–10 million of that already hitting Q1. The bulk of this cost will impact Q2, placing further pressure on already strained margins. These are part of a broader investment-heavy strategy that, while aiming to reposition the company for a low-carbon future, exposes it to substantial short-term risk.

These investments are unfolding in a hostile input cost environment. In Q1 alone, soaring fiber and wood prices inflicted a €131 million negative EBIT impact, especially acute given the company’s Nordic-centric sourcing model. While stabilization is expected later in 2025, the present remains marred by elevated prices that are cutting deeply into profitability.

Stora Enso’s EBIT and EBITDA margins trail sector norms by more than 50%, and its overall profitability grade sits at a mediocre “C.” The company’s return on total capital (ROTC) is only 1.41%, net income margins remain negative at -1.10%, and its asset turnover is in decline. In combination, these indicators suggest that the business is not just low-margin, but struggling to extract efficiency from its asset base.

Against this backdrop, the company’s decision to raise its dividend by 25% year-over-year to a 2.70% yield should invite scrutiny. In healthier firms, dividend hikes signal confidence in cash flows and balance sheet durability. But in Stora Enso’s case here, it feels more like an attempt to pacify investors amid deteriorating fundamentals to me, especially since the company is already reporting negative free cash flow and net losses.

Valuation Breaks from Reality

My take is that these contradictions culminate in a forward-looking valuation model that borders on implausible. Based on aggressive assumptions, including full realization of growth projections and dividend reinvestment, a future share price of $96.66 is projected. That implies nearly a 10x return from current levels, an annualized return of 139%, and total dividends exceeding $1,000. For veteran investors reading this, those kind of figures might resemble early-stage venture capital scenarios for you rather than the outlook for a century-old paper and packaging company facing chronic performance issues and margin pressure.

I think that what makes the valuation condition even more tenuous is the lack of correlation between adjusted operating earnings and the stock price, with a statistically weak -0.84% correlation. Usually, when you have such a rift between fundamentals and market pricing it undermines the case for reversion to the mean and throws into question the reliability of earnings-based valuation models.

Meanwhile, operational weaknesses continue to surface in the company's business segments. The Biomaterials division posted an adjusted EBIT of just €36 million, reflecting not only the drag from softer pulp prices but also the steep cost of wood input. In the Packaging Solutions segment, critical to the company’s bio-transition ambitionsm Stora Enso has only recently returned to profitability after enduring several quarters of losses since Q4 2023.

There’s also the problem of inventory overproduction. In Q1, Stora Enso cranked out more cartonboard than it moved, betting on seasonal demand to catch up. If that demand doesn’t show, or shows up late, they’re stuck holding the bag. Too much stock ties up working capital and raises the odds they’ll have to start cutting prices just to clear it, which means more margin pain heading into Q2.

And then there’s the market itself. Pulp prices are sliding (but showing some signs of recovery), volatility is up, and management says they’ve pushed through price hikes in containerboard, but won’t say by how much. The lack of detail hardly inspires confidence, and it leaves open the question about the company’s pricing power.

Final Takeaway

All things considered, I’m sticking with a Hold on SEOAY. The restructuring, debt paydown, and asset sales are steps in the right direction, but the core problems haven’t gone anywhere. Free cash flow is still negative, Oulu’s ramp-up is dragging earnings, and the long-term underperformance hasn’t been reversed. There’s upside if they execute, but right now, the risk still outweighs the reward.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Lennar: Resilience Built In Its Balance Sheet, But Extreme Caution Warranted
data.description:
In the midst of the soaring home prices, elevated inflation, and tariff woes, the residential property market faces more uncertainty. So, it is not a surprise to see investors stay away from stocks related to the market. Even a known company like Lennar Corporation (NYSE:LEN) experiences the unfavorable impact of these external pressures with its squeezed margins. Yet, it still exudes resilience and survivability with its sound Balance Sheet. In addition, I think risks have already been priced in after its correction, although investors must still be careful when taking a position here.

LEN Q1 2025: Decent Despite Toppish Growth And Cost Pressures

Lennar Corporation sees the combined impact of skyrocketing home prices and sticky inflation. Yet, its strategic management proves it can deliver decent results despite cost pressures that continue to squeeze its margins.

Its Q1 2025 operating revenue amounted to $7.6B, up by 4.4% YoY from $7.3B. Its Q1 revenue has consistently increased for more than a decade, its recovery from the Global Financial Crisis. This was a good aspect to note considering the GFC, the COVID-19 pandemic, and the 2022 hyperinflation. Its strategic transition to an asset-light business model increased its market adaptability and pricing flexibility. Note that it used to own a huge land inventory pre-crisis. Now, it has minimal direct landownership and instead concentrates more on homebuilding and land options.

Its pricing strategy remains one of its key drivers. During Q1 2025, its average sales price was $408,000, down by -1.2% from $413,000. It was also 2.2% lower than the median housing sales price in the US at $416,900. Considering this, it was not surprising to see its deliveries rise by 6.2% from 16,798 to 17,834 units. This offset the impact of lower prices and drove revenue growth.

However, it is important to note that growth has become toppish in recent quarters. In the table below, we can see positive revenue growth since 2012. But from double-digit values, it has already substantially decreased. This shows that its pricing strategy may be approaching its limit and that LEN may have to think of new ways to encourage demand without sacrificing value too much.

On the other hand, LEN could not cushion the impact of inflation as cost pressures continued to take their toll. As a result, its operating margin decreased from 14.8% to 11.1%. Nonetheless, the mere fact that LEN remains profitable despite these challenges shows its ability to sustain its capacity.

Laggards

The residential property market continues to experience skyrocketing prices, although a bit lower than in 2022-2023. Considering this, existing homes remain low after its steep plunge from 2022. In addition, vacancy rates have started increasing again. This can suggest demand issues. To be fair, I think there are enough homes available. The problem is that the available homes either do not match the finances of prospective buyers or are not in an ideal location. In the case of LEN, its pricing strategy is slightly below the median price and remains fruitful as it stays competitive.

The question is how long it can sustain its pricing level with respect to the stubborn inflation. As we can see, its backlog dropped by -19% YoY despite the lower average sales price. We know that inflation has already slowed. However, it's still a positive number, so prices are generally increasing. This is just disinflation, not deflation. So, the cost of homebuilding may still rise. This can be amplified by new tariffs as the price of raw material imports may rise and become inflationary. We must also note that tariffs may affect many industries, so inflation may regain its momentum and disrupt market demand once again. Hence, this may also affect its cost and expense management and pricing strategy.

Risk Mitigants

Despite those potential challenges, I think LEN is capable of surviving or even adapting to it aside from its pricing strategy.

Its Balance Sheet remains well-positioned despite the notable decrease in cash, mainly due to the Millrose Properties, Inc. spin-off. It remains adequate at $2.3B. In addition, its short-term investments are now about twice as much as its value a year before. This shows that LEN finds new ways to earn through investment strategies. Its inventory has also decreased, which could help the company improve its cost management and maintain positive margins.

Moreover, its borrowings have generally decreased in the past 11 quarters, mostly having longer maturities. Its cash alone is already to cover all borrowings at once, showing its high liquidity. In addition, the majority of its borrowings have maturities of more than two years at fixed interest rates. Hence, LEN has enough time and capital buffer to prepare for repayment and is shielded against interest rate hikes.

In a broader view, I don't think the market, including LEN, will crash or a bubble burst as key players remain watchful and careful. They respond to the decreasing home sales by keeping home inventory low. The same can be seen in LEN, which gives it some pricing flexibility. The market also bases home construction and starts on demand. Hence, I believe home prices will stay at their current level, or if not, the decrease will be manageable.

Valuation

LEN has decreased substantially and continuously in the past seven months. I think the bearish response was logical considering the uncertainty surrounding the market and its squeezed income. Its movement appears to mirror its key financial metrics, particularly BVPS and SPS or sales per share. The EPS also reflects it somehow but is less consistent than the two. In its P/B, the stock trades at about 1.25x its book value. It is still higher than the five-year average of 1.22x and the ideal ratio of 1.00x. Considering this, it suggests a TP of $104.99, a -2.8% downside risk. This is contrary to the P/E and P/S, which both show a higher TP of $119.92 and $126.31.

To examine the consistency of the data, I also did a regression analysis. As we can see, all these metrics are highly correlated with the stock price. In addition, all their p-values are very low, even lower than 0.01. This shows that less than 1% of their relationship with price did not happen by chance, erasing data randomness. Yet, it was BVPS that had the highest correlation and the lowest p-value. So, it has the most consistent relationship with the stock price.

While these are all historical numbers, they can help us predict potential price trends or direction. For instance, the 91% correlation shows that if BVPS increases or decreases, there is a 91% probability that the stock price will move in the same direction. So, it is safe to say that the P/B TP is the most ideal. Nonetheless, the idea that the current price is close to it shows that the recent events and potential risks have already been priced in.

Technicals

LEN has become flatter with a slight uptrend after its correction. Yet, it is still below the 50-day and 200-day SMA. Also, the 50-day SMA is still moving below the 200-day SMA. So, the trend does not show strong bullish signals. Instead, sellers no longer accepted any price lower than $100, and buyers found new support after the correction.

MACD and RSI agree with my initial observation. The rebounding momentum also suggests that the recent low became a new support or entry point for buyers. But since the momentum remains consistent with the price trend, we cannot detect any bullish divergence at this point. There's also a bit of consolidation happening since April 22. Hence, holding will be a prudent move.

Conclusion

Lennar Corporation stays resilient with its decent topline. It faces a lot of market challenges right now, but its pricing strategy, prudent inventory management, adequate cash, and manageable borrowings ensure safety and sustainability. In addition, it appears that the recent correction has already priced in its recent performance, outlook, and even market risks. But it's not that cheap yet, and technicals do not still show promise. I am giving this stock a hold rating for now.

data.title: Super Micro Computer: Elephant(s) In The Server Room
data.description:
Investment Thesis

AI server manufacturer Super Micro Computer (NASDAQ:SMCI) remains a flashpoint between bulls and bears in the ongoing debate around appetite for AI and AI server shipments.

With almost a fifth of the company’s shares outstanding shorted, Super Micro Computer is one of the most shorted stocks on the markets.

Bulls, on one hand, argue that forward estimates are too conservative and markets are extremely pessimistic about the company’s shipments of AI servers, especially with Nvidia’s (NASDAQ:NVDA) Blackwell/Blackwell Ultra shipments picking up pace. Then there is that $20B Saudi deal, which sparked some life into the server company’s growth outlook. These are all outcomes that have some likelihood of occurrence in the future.

But I believe there are still a few problems for Super Micro’s management to iron out. The AI server market is notoriously fierce, and the growing presence of Foxconn’s dominance is tough on every other server player. Also, I do not expect the $20B Saudi deal to be revenue-accretive any time soon. Finally, Super Micro also has a Hopper problem, which is showing up in inventory levels.

I believe Super Micro is trading in the fair value range at the moment and reiterate my Hold rating.

Holes In The Bullish Outlook For Super Micro Computer

My previous update on Super Micro Computer delved into the company’s filings that finally arrived in February, earlier this year. I mentioned that Super Micro Computer was able to explain its dealings with its supply chain partners, but I also believed the company was not fully out of the woods due to some inherent problems that still persisted in the company along with the drastically changing nature of the overall market.

In terms of the changing dynamics of the overall server market, I noted the dominant emergence of Foxconn as the leading server player, trumping Super Micro as well as the latter’s other large peer, Dell (NYSE:DELL). In April, a report from Bloomberg detailed how Foxconn was becoming a beneficiary as a result of its supply partnership with Nvidia.

According to the Bloomberg report last month, Foxconn is “riding booming demand for AI servers,” with Q1 sales rising 24.2% “on resilient data center demand.” Foxconn’s own revenue report guided for:

The Cloud and Networking Products segment [AI servers in this segment] is expected to maintain its growth momentum in the second quarter.

It is notable that Foxconn guided for Q2 strength despite Q2 being “traditionally an off-season.”

Compared to Foxconn, Super Micro Computer’s management appeared to backtrack on its $40B FY26 revenue target that it had issued at the start of the year. On the Q3 FY25 call, when Super Micro’s management was asked if they were “reiterating the $40 billion revenue target for fiscal 2026,” management said:

We at this moment do not provide guidance for fiscal year 2026. But when it's the visibility become more clear, we will share at that time.

Agreed that Super Micro will benefit from the ramp of Blackwell/Blackwell Ultra server shipments, which at a later point this year will give management visibility into guiding for FY26 revenue.

However, in the short term, Super Micro is still caught in the crosswinds from customers who are pushing back on all the Hopper server inventory that Super Micro has. This was one of the main reasons why the company’s Q3 revenues of $4.6B fell short of the $5B consensus estimates, per management on the Q3 call.

Moreover, with Super Micro now holding billions of dollars of Hopper inventory, which their customers were rendering obsolete due to the arrival of Blackwell servers, Super Micro was forced to write down much of its Hopper inventory, which resulted in a substantial 220 bp hit to margins in Q3.

Here’s how Super Micro’s gross margin profile has looked since November 2022, the time when ChatGPT was introduced to the world, sparking a revolution in spending for AI infrastructure.

When explaining their stance on gross margins, management talked about taking higher inventory reserves “for some of the older inventory products.” Management is guiding for ~10% gross margins in Q4, which is not a meaningful improvement from Q3.

Bulls have been recently bullish on the $20B deal struck between DataVolt and Super Micro, where the latter will be providing its DLC-based servers to the Saudi-based DataVolt. Over the long haul, this $20B would very well be revenue-accretive, but on a midterm basis, I do not see any revenue contributions from this deal happening any time soon. Why?

DataVolt has recently announced plans for developing two data centers in the ME region. The first is a planned small server facility in Riyadh. The second planned data center is much larger, with projected capacity to be ~1.5 GW in the new NEOM industrial city. I expect DataVolt’s 1.5 GW planned data center to account for a major chunk of the $20B deal with Super Micro.

However, with the first 300 MW of the 1.5 GW planned data center to go live reportedly only by 2028, I do not expect Super Micro to see any meaningful bumps in revenue from the $20B deal over the next 12-15 months at least.

Super Micro Trades In The Fair Value Range

With Super Micro’s Q4 FY25 almost upon us, investors will benefit from evaluating the company’s FY26 outlook.

As seen in IDC’s Q4 CY24 Server Market report, Super Micro posted 55% y/y growth in the December quarter last year, slightly better than the total revenues of all other OEM vendors such as Dell, HPE (NYSE:HPE), etc.

With IDC projecting server revenues to grow closer to the mid-twenties this year (see:Non-x86 Annual Growth %), markets are marginally optimistic that Super Micro will outgrow its OEM peers, with consensus estimates calling for 35% revenue growth in FY26.

On the EPS front, Super Micro is expected to report FY26 EPS growth of 39%. Between the periods of March ‘23 and March ‘25, TTM diluted EPS for Super Micro grew by 30-31% CAGR, for which markets valued Super Micro at 10-11x forward earnings.

Currently, if Super Micro’s FY26 earnings outlook is projected to grow by 39%, the company should be valued at no more than 13-14x, which makes Super Micro look marginally overvalued. This valuation implies Super Micro’s stock should be valued at ~$39 per share based on my mid-term outlook.

Investors should also note that this outlook is based on Super Micro’s revenue and EPS estimates, which have been revised continuously lower by analysts this year. Expect more turbulence if this trend of downward revisions continues.

What could help boost the outlook is if Super Micro could expand the scope of its AI server shipments either by winning more partnerships and/or deepening its relationship further with Nvidia.

Nvidia’s results from last night will surely boost the stock, but the longer the stock runs, the farther it gets away from the current outlook and its business fundamentals, in my opinion.

Takeaway

While Super Micro bulls have reasonable arguments to support their positive outlook, I recommend caution over the next few months due to the wide range of headwinds that continue to persist for the AI server company.

Competitive headwinds, inventory turnover, and lack of securing additional sales commitments will only complicate this further for Super Micro at a fundamental level. But since the company’s shares outstanding are one of the most shorted, any small positive developments can create price distortions in its stock price.

At 14-15x FY26 earnings, Super Micro trades just above the higher end of its multiple range. I recommend staying Neutral on the company.

data.title: It's Time To Dine And Dash With DoorDash, Given High Valuation And Competitive Risks
data.description:
Thesis: Rising inflation and the risks of an economic slowdown could see customers shifting away from expensive delivery services, making DASH's sky-high valuation metrics tough to swallow. As such, DASH is a sell for me.

I’ve already written about this at length, but currently we’re seeing signs that American households are increasingly experiencing financial duress. I’ll summarize my findings below, but if you want to go more in-depth, check out this article.

We’re seeing clear signs that many Americans are in a financially tough spot after years of relatively high inflation. Credit card delinquencies are on the rise, more people are falling behind on car payments, and bankruptcies are becoming more common.

During times of financial duress, many families will tighten their spending and look for the best value (more on that in a moment). For example, the more affordable fast food options may be able to maintain revenues, but more expensive sit-down chains may see less traffic. Consumers will also put more effort into hunting for sales and promotions.

While the economy overall has proven resilient and recession risks have retreated, the prospect of economic turbulence can’t be ignored, and it’s wise for investors to pay close attention. Should the economy stall, consumers may cut back spending.

With the above in mind, I believe that DoorDash (NYSE:DASH) and meal delivery in general could suffer diminishing demand as customers look to avoid the expensive fees and tips. If so, DASH will struggle to maintain and improve upon its already high P/E ratio. That said, it’s not all bad news, and DoorDash also has a variety of strengths, such as high Gross Profit Margins.

Why DoorDash May End Up on the Consumer’s Chopping Block

Researchers studying the Great Recession and its impact on consumers have found that many households shifted focus to value, putting more time and resources into hunting for good deals. In many cases, individuals were essentially substituting non-paid work for paid work after being laid off. Many consumers reduced eating at restaurants or enjoying takeout and instead bought groceries to cook food at home.

DoorDash’s high fees could quickly drive away customers, as for most households, delivery services are more of a luxury than a necessity. (In some cases, for people who can’t afford a car or suffer limited mobility, delivery may be a necessity.) If folks get laid off or see their hours cut, many will likely once again substitute paid work for non-paid work, which will probably equate to picking up food rather than paying for it to be delivered.

Even if a recession doesn’t set in, years of high inflation, expensive housing costs, potential tax increases, and various other factors will encourage customers to cut spending. While customers have been willing to pay extra for DoorDash, economic necessity, or at least prudence, may shift demand away.

We are already seeing signs that customers are shifting away from more expensive restaurant chains. McDonald’s (NYSE:MCD), which has dramatically raised prices in recent years, well above most other fast food restaurants, has been suffering from declining sales. Chipotle Mexican Grill (NYSE:CMG), which has come under fire for high prices and small portions, has also seen sales drop in recent months. Chili’s, owned by Brinker International (NYSE:EAT), meanwhile, has seen revenues soar, due in large part to their $11.99 three for me promotion.

Circling back to DoorDash, a single meal delivery typically costs between $1.99 and $5.99. Further, many drivers will expect a tip and may skip orders that don’t provide a tip. Customers can also opt to join DashPass, paying $9.99 per month to reduce fees. User fees are not the only cost, however. Restaurants also have to fork over 15 to 30% of the bill. Restaurants, in turn, often pass the costs onto customers. It’s not uncommon for food to be more expensive in the app than it is on the menu at the restaurant. One Redditor actually went out and documented the price differences, as seen below.

The above case is somewhat anecdotal, and so far, I’ve failed to find any larger surveys or studies. Yet pretty much anyone who’s used DoorDash regularly will know that the mark-ups can be quite steep. That said, so far, DoorDash has continued to enjoy strong growth. In Q1 2025, the company saw orders increase by 18%. There might be a slight slowdown in order growth, although it appears to be negligible for now.

High Profitability Might be a Double-Edged Sword

High profits are good, right? The answer, of course, is ‘of course!’ DASH’s 50.24% Gross Profit Margin (TTM) is certainly appealing and is substantially higher than the industry median of 38.33%. Yet, high profit potential will also attract competition. While DASH is currently the leader in meal delivery in the United States, Uber Eats is putting up a strong challenge, and we can’t rule out that a new entrant could bring some sort of competitive or innovative edge.

Globally, many other companies quickly recognized the potential for delivery services, and in many markets, there is at least one strong delivery service provider. Often, you’ll find multiple competitive companies. As a result, DoorDash may struggle to grow outside of the four countries it currently operates in: the U.S., Canada, Australia, and New Zealand. In the United States, roughly half of Canadians reported using DoorDash in the last year. While DASH is generally performing well in these markets, the company is also a market share leader in the United States.

Acquisitions could open markets, but they can get pricey, and established competitors may have a better feel for the market/society, etc. While DoorDash is more popular in the United States than Uber Eats, in international markets, the latter often holds the edge. Grab and Food Panda, meanwhile, are very popular across Asia, and several delivery providers already operate in China.

The good news is that high profit margins could give DASH some room to offer promotions and discounts, should consumers and restaurants start to balk at fees. Still, delivery will likely remain expensive.

DoorDash Valuation and Fundamentals

As for DASH’s valuation, I first turn to GuruFocus’s DCF calculator, which can be used to conduct a reverse DCF calculation. With the discount rate set at 10% and the terminal growth rate pegged at 4%, in line with generally observed worldwide GDP growth rates in recent years, I find that DASH will need to sustain EPS growth rates in excess of 50%. This calculator uses an EPS w/o NRI calculation by default (the theory being that EPS is a more reliable indicator of share prices).

Switching to FCF measurements, the growth rate is more realistic at 24.5%. In both cases, however, I believe it will be difficult for DASH to maintain such growth, especially if the economy contracts or inflation rises, forcing households to more tightly manage their finances.

As with many hot tech stocks, DASH’s P/E ratio is pretty tough to swallow at the moment, weighing in at ~53 (TTM non-GAAP) vs a sector media of 14.7 (Seeking Alpha data). The P/E GAAP (TTM) is even worse, at 266 vs a sector median of 18.5. High P/E ratios are the name of the game right now, but these numbers are well above most of the Magnificent 7 and other big-name tech stocks.

DASH is doing well as far as growth is concerned, helping explain the high P/E ratio. According to Seeking Alpha’s data, the company is currently enjoying growth of 23.35% YOY vs. a sector median of 2.3%. Revenue (TTM) now tops $11.2 billion, making such strong growth especially appealing. DoorDash is no small startup going through its growth stage, but is instead a large, established company.

As mentioned earlier, DASH’s Gross Profit Margin (TTM) weighs in at 50.24%, well above the sector median of 38.44%. Yet, the operating margin is a mere 3%, versus a sector median of 4.48%. On the whole, while DASH’s fundamentals are generally strong, the valuation is too expensive in the face of the considerable risks. Seeking Alpha’s quant tools likewise give DASH poor marks in the valuation department.

My Takeaway: DASH Presents Too Many Risks and Not Enough Upside

I wouldn’t necessarily rush to sell DASH if it were in my portfolio. Given the strong fundamentals, it’d be relatively easy to classify the stock as a hold in and of itself. That said, financially distressed consumers will likely create headwinds, and for many people, food delivery is textbook discretionary. Should a recession set in, DASH will come under even more intense pressure.

While DASH may continue to hold ground or even notch gains in the weeks ahead, I don't believe the upside justifies the risks. As such, DASH is a sell for me. I do believe the company itself is fundamentally sound, and would reconsider my position should the valuation become more attractive in the future.

While I'm not looking to jump into the delivery market, if it were a sector I was interested in right now, Uber (NYSE:UBER) with its Uber Eats program would be worth a closer look. Not only does UBER have a much more attractive P/E ratio, but efforts in autonomous vehicles could yield cost savings and bolster competitiveness in the long run.

data.title: Navigator Holdings: Possibly The Cheapest Valuation In The Company's History
data.description:
Thesis

In Q1, Navigator Holdings (NYSE:NVGS) reported another quarter of consistent performance, generating $72.8 million in EBITDA. This strong performance was mainly attributed to record time charter rates for the company’s vessels which exceeded $30,000/d for the first time in over a decade.

Despite the company’s consistent EBITDA generation over the last two years, the stock now trades at the lowest valuation in nearly a decade. The irony here is that the company has never been more profitable or with a stronger balance sheet than it has today. NVGS plans to capitalize on this extremely cheap valuation by authorizing a special $50 million buyback program that is in addition to the company’s standard return of capital policy.

Through this program, NVGS is capable of retiring over 5% of the total outstanding shares. This organic form of EPS growth will provide significant long-term value for shareholders in the years leading up to the receipt of its four medium gas carrier vessels. As a result, I am reiterating my BUY rating for NVGS.

Q1 Earnings Review

NVGS reported $72.8 million of EBITDA for the first quarter, which was largely in line with Q4. However, most notably, this performance came despite a rather significant step change in performance from the company’s ethylene terminal (owned through a JV with Enterprise Products (EPD)).

After reporting several years of consistent EBITDA generation, the terminal was negatively affected by low domestic supplies of ethylene, limiting export cargos. Now that several major turnarounds are complete for domestic ethylene crackers, NVGS has observed volumes quickly return to near full capacity at the beginning of the second quarter.

NVGS was able to mitigate the negative impacts of the terminal’s performance through strong time charter rates on the shipping side of the business. Time charter equivalent rates exceeded $30,000/d for the first time in over a decade. The company stated that this was largely driven by improved terms on its time charter contracts, which makes this level of profitability significantly more durable moving forward.

Q2 Outlook Still Appears Strong

As noted above, NVGS has consistently generated between $68 and $78 million in EBITDA for the last nine quarters. Despite a heavy quarter of dry dockings, I expect the company will again deliver strong results in this range.

The additional expenses and lost revenues associated with the heavy dry docking schedule will likely result in $9-$11 million negative impact on Q2 EBITDA. However, now that the ethylene terminal is operating near full capacity, I expect a significant portion of this loss to be offset by a return to normal earning levels.

This will be further aided by the receipt of minimum volume commitment payments that the company is owed despite the low terminal throughput in Q1. All together, I expect NVGS will report in the range of $68-$72 million of EBITDA in Q2.

Valuation

As I have discussed in previous articles for NVGS, the company supports rather strong leverage metrics. Leverage now sits at just 2.6x TTM EBITDA, putting the company in a very secure position. The company has been able to achieve this by consistently deleveraging over the last five years while also consistently growing EBITDA.

Simultaneous to this effort, the company has also retired nearly 10% of the total outstanding shares since early 2023. The trifecta of paying down debt, buying back shares and responsibly growing EBITDA have now driven the company’s valuation to historically low levels.

In the chart below, we can see that NVGS now trades at just 6.6x on an EV:EBITDA basis. This is the lowest level the company has traded at since late 2016 when the stock traded for under $10/share.

Despite the similar valuation, the business is significantly more investable today than it was back in 2016.

  • NVGS was formerly highly leveraged (>6x).
  • The company did not pay a dividend in 2016 (or repurchase shares).
  • The Morgan’s Point export terminal did not exist.
  • In late 2016, time charter rates dropped into the low $20,000/day range due to an oversupplied market.

With this historical context in mind, let's add a few more details regarding the company’s current position.

  • The company recently completed the expansion of the Morgan’s Point export terminal.
  • In Q4 2024, NVGS commenced a newbuild program of four 48,000 cubic meters medium gas carriers. These vessels are expected to be partially financed by free cash flow and may provide a significant step change in earnings when they are delivered.
  • The US petrochemical industry will likely absorb market share in the coming years, as many European ethylene crackers are expected to close due to stressed economics.

By taking these points together, I believe that not only is NVGS cheaply valued, but it may also be the cheapest the stock has ever been.

Risks

NVGS is primarily a shipping stock which is susceptible to changes in profitability resulting from an influx in vessel supply. As mentioned earlier, a similar event occurred nearly 10 years ago when an influx of new vessels reduced rates from over $30,000/d to the low $20,000/d range.

Currently, the order book for the handy-sized fleet sits at just 9% while over 20% of the fleet is greater than 20 years old. In the near term, this appears to be a positive development for the company. However, as day rates continue to slowly improve, additional capital will inevitably be drawn to the market.

Unfortunately, NVGS has zero control over this risk. Investors should maintain a cautious eye on the order book for early signs of an oversupplied shipping market.

Investor Key Takeaways

  1. Q1 reported yet another quarter of consistent EBITDA generation.
  2. Despite challenges associated with a heavy drydocking schedule, Q2 should also report strong EBITDA thanks to a rebound in export volumes via the Morgan’s Point export terminal.
  3. Based on pure numbers, you would have to go back 10 years to find a time when NVGS traded as cheaply as it does today. However, when factoring in the significant improvements the business has made over the last decade it is no comparison. NVGS has simply never been a better value than it is today.
  4. As with most shipping stocks, one of the largest risks is the potential for an oversupplied market. For the time being, the order book remains advantageous for shippers. However, investors would be wise to continually monitor for any changes in this area.
